title: Gold: Dubious Speculation

extracted_rules: Now, what's interesting is when you look at monthly candles for gold, I don't think you could look at this and be able to definitively say that the bull market has to be over, which has been the thesis that we've been talking about that yes, gold did get ahead of itself.
Even if the top for silver is in, even in the case where silver has topped, gold could still go to an all-time high.
Now, if silver has topped for the year, I do not I do not look at this chart and believe that it's like a top that will last for say 20 years.

I would say for silver, if this is the top for the year, I might envision us sort of in in a top like this where we had in in 1974 where it might fall off for a while, but I would expect it to come back up within, you know, a few years.

And if you look at what silver did in 1973 or sorry 1974, you can see that it dropped about 43% and kind of consolidated with some lower highs for a while, but then eventually it built back up into another bull run.
And when you look at gold or sorry when you look at silver in this move you can see that the correction that it just had was around 47%.
Now one of the things we mentioned for silver is that it should get a counter trend rally that kind of tops out in the March April May time frame if history is any indication.
If you look at at silver, if we overlay the price of silver with the price of gold, and we're just going to look back in history and kind of see how these two interact with each other.

For instance, if you were to look at the S&P 500 divided by gold, so the valuation of the S&P against gold and you look at where it just broke down from, you'll see that that's exactly where it held support at in the60s before then breaking down in 1973, right in the 1970s.
And in fact, if this were to play out like the the last two major bull markets in gold, you can see there's a couple of them.

Now, one of the hard parts about this is that a lot of people will have gold in their portfolio, but then if it drops, let's say 40 50% like it did in the 1970s, or if it drops 30% like it did in the financial crisis, they might then panic and sell.
Even if you FOMOed in at the top, even if you still were up 350% six years later, even if you bought at the top of the financial crisis, you still were up about 88% just a few years later.
A lot of people, if you're in crypto, for instance, and and you you trade meme coins, your time scale, your horizon on your investments might be about four or five minutes.
If that's the case, then I really would not recommend um you know, weathering the the heavy metal verse because this stuff takes a much longer period of time.
So that's and by the way when it broke down in 1973, gold continued to go up, right?
But when you look at this and you were to overlay the S&P 500 onto this chart, this is this is really critical.
it's that if the US does go into a recession within the next couple years, it'll likely take a lot longer for stocks to get to new all-time highs than it would for gold.
The stock market topped in 1973 approximately, maybe late, yeah, around 1973, maybe late 1972, I don't know exactly, but I think it was like late early 1973 when there was a recession.
We're saying that if it does, okay, there's no free lunch with investing, right?
If you're going to buy, you have to be prepared for the downside.

And I do sometimes wonder if if this is a trend line that's going to come into play as well, where, you know, you might see it bounce around here for a little while.
